ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general fried chicken food product entry; no specific 2-piece with coleslaw composite entry exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP covers fried chicken as a category; the coleslaw side component is not separately coded in this composite record. Closest defensible anchor used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for this composite fried chicken with coleslaw set meal. Null applied honestly.

serving.description: 1 set (2 pieces of fried chicken with coleslaw)
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving consists of 2 pieces of deep-fried chicken (approximately 250–280 g combined) accompanied by a side of coleslaw (approximately 70–100 g), totalling around 350 g per set meal as commonly sold at fast-food outlets and hawker stalls in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Fried chicken (2 pc) with coleslaw — Singapore HPB Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id F08020020 is sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo with confidence 0.95 and review_status reviewed. All per_100g values are taken from this canonical HPB entry. Saturated fat estimated at 3.5 g/100g based on HPB compositional data for similar fried chicken preparations; monounsaturated, polyunsaturated, trans fat, potassium, cholesterol, vitamins, and other micronutrients set to -1 as not individually reported in the HPB entry for this composite dish.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate based on battered/breaded fried chicken (GI ~45) from University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this specific composite dish (2-piece fried chicken with coleslaw). GI of 45 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for battered/breaded fried chicken, which is the dominant carbohydrate-contributing component. Coleslaw contributes minimal carbohydrate and does not materially alter the GI. GL per serving calculated as: (GI 45 × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (45 × 22.5) / 100 ≈ 10.1, based on 350 g serving with 6.42 g carbohydrate per 100 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 436 mg/100 g with insufficient potassium offset
hypertension.guidance: At 436 mg sodium per 100 g, a 350 g serving delivers approximately 1,526 mg sodium — over 60% of the 2,400 mg daily limit — making this dish unsuitable for regular consumption by individuals with hypertension; request reduced-salt preparation and limit portion size.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low GI (~45) and modest carbohydrate load offset by saturated fat from deep-frying
type2_diabetes.guidance: The low GI (~45) and modest carbohydrate content (6.42 g/100 g) are favourable for glycaemic control, but the saturated fat from deep-frying (~3.5 g/100 g) can worsen insulin resistance over time; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit frequency and request grilled chicken where possible.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium, saturated fat from deep-frying, and potential trans fat from frying oil
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The combination of high sodium (436 mg/100 g), saturated fat (~3.5 g/100 g), and potential trans fat from repeated frying oil use poses a significant cardiovascular risk; individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid this dish or substitute with grilled chicken and a vinegar-dressed coleslaw.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that promote joint inflammation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Deep-fried chicken is a significant source of dietary AGEs which exacerbate joint inflammation in rheumatoid arthritis; individuals with this condition should substitute with grilled or poached chicken and retain the coleslaw component for its cruciferous anti-inflammatory benefit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fried Chicken (2 pieces) with Coleslaw is a popular set meal widely available at fast-food chains, hawker centres, and food courts across Singapore. The dish consists of two pieces of bone-in chicken — typically a drumstick and a thigh or breast — coated in a seasoned wheat-flour batter and deep-fried until golden and crispy. The coleslaw side dish is made from shredded cabbage and carrot bound with a mayonnaise-based dressing, providing a cool, creamy contrast to the hot, crunchy chicken. Per 100 g, the dish provides 182 kcal, 13.5 g protein, 11.38 g fat, and 6.42 g carbohydrate, with sodium at 436 mg — a clinically significant level for hypertension management. The GI is estimated at approximately 45 (low), driven by the battered coating, with a glycaemic load of approximately 10.1 per 350 g serving. The dish is high in protein, making it satiating, but the deep-frying method introduces saturated fat and potential advanced glycation end-products (AGEs), which are relevant concerns for cardiovascular disease, insulin resistance, and inflammatory conditions such as rheumatoid arthritis. The coleslaw contributes dietary fibre (0.94 g/100 g), vitamin C from cabbage, and some potassium, partially offsetting the sodium load. This dish is commonly consumed as a working lunch or casual dinner and is typically halal-certified at major fast-food outlets in Singapore. It is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans. Individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease should limit portion size and frequency due to the high sodium and saturated fat content.

Is fried chicken with coleslaw good for weight loss?

confidence_reason: Core nutritional values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F08020020, confidence 0.95, review_status reviewed). This is a canonical primary regional source for Singapore food records. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for battered fried chicken, which is the closest defensible approved-source match. Saturated fat is estimated from HPB compositional data for similar fried chicken preparations.
notes: G3 sentinel-value documentation: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are all set to -1 because detailed fatty acid fractions, full micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this composite fried chicken with coleslaw set meal are not individually reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for cr_id F08020020 as of 2026; the HPB source was consulted and confirmed these fields are not available for this specific composite dish record. Saturated fat estimated at 3.5 g/100g based on HPB compositional data for comparable fried chicken preparations in the Singapore food composition database; this is an adapted value and not directly stated in the source entry. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for battered/breaded fried chicken; no direct GI measurement exists for this specific composite dish. Serving size of 350 g is an estimate based on typical fast-food portion sizes for a 2-piece fried chicken set meal in Singapore; no standardised portion weight is recorded in the HPB source entry. All core macros (protein_g 13.5, fat_g 11.38, carbohydrate_g 6.42) are non-zero and sourced from HPB; no zero-macro justification is required.
